items.title: Trump signs executive order on regenerative agriculture
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on regenerative agriculture, and USDA announced a related Regenerative Feedstock Rule.
items.details: On June 25, 2026, President Trump signed an executive order directing federal agencies to expand support for regenerative agriculture and precision farming. USDA Secretary Brooke Rollins announced an accompanying Regenerative Feedstock Rule connecting certain crops to biofuel supply-chain markets.

items.title: Executive order on post-quantum cryptography signed
items.summary: The White House issued an executive order directing federal systems to transition to NIST-approved post-quantum cryptography standards.
items.details: Signed June 22, 2026, the order states U.S. policy to move federal information systems to NIST-approved Federal Information Processing Standards for post-quantum cryptography and to assist critical infrastructure operators with their transitions, citing the threat posed by future large-scale quantum computers.

items.title: GAO reports federal workforce decline in 2025
items.summary: A GAO report found the federal workforce at 22 major agencies declined by more than 11% from December 2024 to January 2026.
items.details: Published June 17, 2026, the GAO report stated nearly 378,000 employees separated from these agencies during 2025 while about 127,000 were hired. The total workforce fell by nearly 256,000 employees, with declines ranging from about 1% at DHS to over 45% at the Department of Education.

items.title: U.S. and Iran exchange strikes amid fragile ceasefire
items.summary: U.S. forces struck Iranian military targets and Iran launched munitions at U.S. facilities in Bahrain and Kuwait, straining a June ceasefire memorandum.
items.details: U.S. Central Command said it hit 10 Iranian military targets after an Iranian attack on a commercial ship in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran said its Revolutionary Guard struck U.S.-linked sites in Kuwait and Bahrain in retaliation. Both sides accused the other of violating the June 17 memorandum.

items.title: Israel and Lebanon sign U.S.-brokered framework agreement
items.summary: Israel and Lebanon signed a U.S.-brokered framework agreement described as a first step toward ending hostilities.
items.details: Announced June 26, 2026, the framework calls for Israeli forces to withdraw from two areas of southern Lebanon. Hezbollah's main political ally, the Amal movement, rejected direct negotiations with Israel, and disputes continued over conditions for a withdrawal and disarmament.

items.title: Congress passes housing affordability bill
items.summary: Congress passed the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, described as the largest federal housing affordability measure in decades.
items.details: The Senate passed H.R. 6644 and the House followed by a 358-32 vote. The bill streamlines federal financing regulations, creates a grant program for preapproved housing designs, and addresses manufactured-home rules. President Trump canceled a planned signing ceremony, saying Congress should first pass voter ID legislation.

items.title: Second reconciliation bill funds ICE and CBP
items.summary: A second budget reconciliation bill providing $70 billion for ICE and CBP was passed and signed into law.
items.details: The House passed S.2, referred to as 'Reconciliation 2.0,' on June 9 by a 214-212 party-line vote after the Senate cleared it June 5. The bill was signed into law the day after House passage and used the budget reconciliation process, which allows Senate passage with a simple majority.

items.title: Supreme Court allows end of TPS for Haiti and Syria
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Mullin v. Doe that the administration can end Temporary Protected Status designations for Haiti and Syria.
items.details: On June 25, 2026, the Court paused lower-court rulings that had blocked ending the designations, holding that federal law generally bars judicial review of TPS termination decisions. The majority also found challengers unlikely to succeed on claims that the terminations were racially discriminatory.

items.title: Supreme Court clears revival of border asylum 'metering'
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 to allow the administration to revive a policy limiting daily asylum applications at the U.S.-Mexico border.
items.details: In Mullin v. Al Otro Lado, decided June 25, 2026, the Court overturned a lower-court order blocking the practice. The majority held that migrants stopped before crossing have not 'arrived' to apply for asylum. Justice Sotomayor dissented from the bench.

items.title: Court strikes down Hawaii firearm-on-property law
items.summary: In Wolford v. Lopez, the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that states cannot require gun owners to obtain advance permission to carry firearms onto private property.
items.details: Decided June 25, 2026, the Court held that requiring advance permission is an undue burden on the right to carry a firearm. The ruling affects laws in states including Hawaii, California, Maryland, New York, and New Jersey.

items.title: Judge blocks parts of Trump election executive order
items.summary: A federal judge blocked key provisions of President Trump's executive order affecting mail voting in the 2026 midterms.
items.details: On June 25, 2026, Judge Indira Talwani ruled that the federal government lacks authority to create centralized lists of adult citizens or give the U.S. Postal Service authority over who may vote by mail. The order was the second election-related executive order issued by Trump.

items.title: Texas redistricting map upheld for 2026 midterms
items.summary: The Supreme Court left in place Texas's new congressional map, ensuring it will be used in the 2026 midterm elections.
items.details: The ruling maintained the map drawn in 2025 after President Trump urged the state to add Republican-leaning seats. Several civil rights groups had challenged the map as racially discriminatory. California and Virginia had approved maps aimed at countering Texas's changes.

items.title: Supreme Court narrows Voting Rights Act in Louisiana case
items.summary: In Louisiana v. Callais, the Court held that maps maximizing minority candidates' chances can violate the Equal Protection Clause, narrowing Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act.
items.details: The 6-3 ruling held that states run afoul of Section 2 only if they intentionally draw districts to give minority voters less opportunity because of race. The decision prompted several states to reconsider their congressional maps ahead of the 2026 midterms.

CA.title: Newsom signs election-protection law SB 73
CA.summary: Governor Newsom signed Senate Bill 73 strengthening California's election safeguards against interference and intimidation.
CA.details: Signed May 27, 2026, the law adds protections for voters and election workers and limits unauthorized law enforcement activity at polling places. The state has separately announced Proposition 64 grants and an AI job-loss tracking tool in late June 2026.

OH.title: DeWine signs bills, issues vetoes
OH.summary: Governor Mike DeWine signed several bills into law and issued line-item and full vetoes on June 24, 2026.
OH.details: Among the bills signed were Senate Bill 19 on academic intervention services and House Bill 170 establishing regulation of carbon capture and storage. DeWine issued a line-item veto in appropriations bill HB 479 and vetoed HB 173 concerning submetered utility services.

FL.title: DeSantis signs local property tax legislation
FL.summary: Governor DeSantis signed Senate Bill 4-F and House Bill 1329 addressing local property taxes and government accountability.
FL.details: Announced around June 25, 2026, SB 4-F strengthens safeguards against local property tax increases and serves as the implementation bill for a proposed constitutional amendment to be considered by voters in November 2026.
FL.source: Executive Office of the Governor of Florida

CT.title: Lamont signs youth online safety and AI law
CT.summary: Governor Ned Lamont signed Public Act 26-15 establishing youth online safety protections and AI regulations.
CT.details: Signed June 2, 2026, the bipartisan law addresses youth social media use, sets requirements around artificial intelligence including in hiring, and creates workforce training initiatives. State officials said it codifies protections amid federal inaction.

NH.title: Ayotte signs 18 bills, issues vetoes
NH.summary: Governor Kelly Ayotte signed 18 bills into law and issued several vetoes the week of June 12, 2026.
NH.details: Signed measures included HB 1381 extending the party filing period and HB 1549 on vehicle registration documents. Ayotte vetoed HB 1072 regarding Department of Labor wage authority, HB 1643 on guardians ad litem, and SB 468 on marijuana cultivation.

IA.title: Reynolds signs final bills of 2026 session
IA.summary: Governor Kim Reynolds signed the final bills from the 2026 legislative session, including a measure on gubernatorial powers.
IA.details: On June 2, 2026, Reynolds acted on remaining bills in her final year as governor, signing dozens of measures. Coverage noted a law restricting certain gubernatorial powers and Republican-backed appropriations and policy bills enacted during the session.

VA.title: Supreme Court ends Virginia redistricting effort
VA.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court rejected Virginia Democrats' emergency appeal, ending the state's mid-decade redistricting effort for 2026.
VA.details: The Friday ruling left in place the congressional districts adopted in 2021 after the Virginia Supreme Court struck down a redistricting referendum in May. Several Democratic candidates suspended campaigns, and both parties regrouped to compete under the existing map.

LA.title: Louisiana redraws map after Supreme Court ruling
LA.summary: Louisiana suspended its congressional primary to redraw district maps following the Supreme Court's Louisiana v. Callais decision.
LA.details: Governor Jeff Landry suspended the state's primary so the legislature could draw a new map compliant with the ruling. The decision arose from a challenge to a map that had created a second majority-minority district.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources and does not endorse any party, official, or position.
